name: Email Draft
description: writes a clear email from a rough intent or bullet points
when_to_use: when the user wants to send an email but only has a goal or scattered notes
body:
1. nail the one outcome you want before writing a word.
2. lead with that ask or news in the first sentence, not the third.
3. give only the context needed to act, cut the backstory.
4. write a subject line that says what it is and what's needed.
5. close with a concrete next step and a date.
6. read it once as the recipient, delete anything they don't need.

name: Reply Diplomatically
description: drafts a measured reply to a tense or loaded message
when_to_use: when the user got an email or message that annoyed them and wants to respond without escalating
body:
1. don't reply from the first emotion, name what actually went wrong.
2. acknowledge their point in one honest line before pushing back.
3. state your position as facts, not accusations ("the deadline moved" not "you moved it").
4. swap every "you always/never" for a specific instance.
5. offer one path forward so it's not just defense.
6. cut sarcasm, exclamation marks, and anything you'd regret forwarded.

name: Say No Gracefully
description: declines a request without burning the relationship
when_to_use: when the user needs to turn down an invite, ask, or request and wants to keep it clean
body:
1. say no in the first line, no long windup that fakes a maybe.
2. give one real reason, not three excuses.
3. don't apologize more than once.
4. offer an alternative only if you mean it (later date, a referral, partial help).
5. keep it warm and short, length reads as guilt.
6. don't leave a door open you plan to keep shut.

name: Follow Up
description: nudges a stalled thread without sounding pushy
when_to_use: when the user sent something and got silence and wants to chase a reply
body:
1. reply on the original thread so context rides along.
2. assume they're busy, not ignoring you, and say so lightly.
3. restate the single thing you need in one line.
4. make saying yes trivial: a yes/no, a date, a one-click option.
5. add a soft deadline ("if i don't hear by friday i'll assume X").
6. cap it at three lines, this is a nudge not a resend.

name: Cold Outreach
description: writes a first-contact message to a stranger that gets a reply
when_to_use: when the user wants to email or dm someone who doesn't know them
body:
1. open with why them specifically, prove you didn't mass-send it.
2. say who you are in one clause, not a resume.
3. make the ask small and concrete, a 15-min call beats "pick your brain."
4. give them an easy out so it doesn't feel like pressure.
5. keep the whole thing under 120 words, long cold emails die.
6. no flattery they've heard a hundred times, be specific or skip it.

name: Meeting Agenda
description: turns a fuzzy meeting purpose into a tight agenda
when_to_use: when the user is calling a meeting and needs structure so it doesn't drift
body:
1. write the one decision or outcome the meeting must produce.
2. if there's no decision to make, kill the meeting and send an email.
3. list 2-4 topics, each with an owner and a time box.
4. mark each item: decide, discuss, or inform, so people know the mode.
5. attach what to read beforehand so the meeting isn't a read-aloud.
6. end the agenda with the desired end-state in one line.

name: Notes To Actions
description: converts messy meeting notes into owned action items
when_to_use: when the user has raw notes or a transcript and needs the to-dos pulled out
body:
1. read the whole thing before extracting anything.
2. pull every decision made and write it as a flat statement.
3. pull every action: who does what by when, drop ones with no owner or flag them.
4. separate decisions from actions from open questions, three lists.
5. cut discussion that led nowhere, keep only outcomes.
6. end with the single most important next step up top.

name: Give Feedback
description: delivers critical feedback that lands without crushing
when_to_use: when the user needs to tell someone something isn't working
body:
1. anchor on the specific behavior or work, never the person's character.
2. say what you observed, then the impact it had, then what you want instead.
3. give one or two examples, not a pile of grievances.
4. drop the compliment-sandwich, it muffles the real point.
5. make the path forward concrete and doable.
6. end by inviting their side, feedback is a conversation not a verdict.

name: Ask For Feedback
description: requests feedback in a way that gets useful answers
when_to_use: when the user wants real critique on their work, not polite nods
body:
1. ask about one specific thing, "any thoughts?" gets you nothing.
2. tell them what kind of feedback you want: blunt, directional, line-level.
3. give them an easy on-ramp ("what's the weakest part?").
4. say where you're already unsure so they aim there.
5. make it safe to be harsh, say you want the real version.
6. thank them for the sharp stuff specifically, that's how you get more.

name: Apologize Well
description: writes a real apology that repairs instead of deflects
when_to_use: when the user messed up and needs to own it in writing
body:
1. name the specific thing you did, no "if anyone was affected."
2. own it without "but" or a reason that's really an excuse.
3. say you get the impact it had on them.
4. state what you're doing so it doesn't repeat.
5. don't fish for reassurance, the apology isn't about your guilt.
6. keep it short, a long apology asks them to comfort you.

name: Status Update
description: writes a crisp progress update people actually read
when_to_use: when the user needs to report where a project or task stands
body:
1. lead with one line: on track, at risk, or blocked.
2. say what shipped since last update in plain done/not-done terms.
3. flag blockers with the exact help you need and from whom.
4. give next steps with dates, not "soon."
5. skip the activity log, report outcomes not effort.
6. keep it scannable, bold the status, bullets for the rest.

name: Difficult Conversation
description: scripts the opening for a hard talk so it starts right
when_to_use: when the user is dreading a confrontation and needs a way in
body:
1. write the one thing this conversation has to resolve.
2. open by naming the topic and that it's hard, don't ambush.
3. lead with the shared goal so it's not you-versus-them.
4. state your view as one perspective, then genuinely ask for theirs.
5. prep two phrases to de-escalate if it heats up.
6. know your walk-away line before you start, not mid-fight.

name: Summarize Thread
description: condenses a long email or chat thread into the state of play
when_to_use: when the user is buried in a thread and needs the gist plus what's owed
body:
1. read top to bottom before summarizing, threads reverse on themselves.
2. state the current decision or status in one line up top.
3. list what's been agreed vs still open.
4. flag who owes what next.
5. drop the back-and-forth, keep only what changed the outcome.
6. end with the single action needed to move it forward.

name: Chat Tone Shift
description: rewrites a message to fit the right register for the audience
when_to_use: when the user has a draft that's too stiff, too casual, or off for the reader
body:
1. name the reader and the relationship, that sets the register.
2. decide the target: warmer, more formal, more direct, softer.
3. cut hedging ("just", "maybe", "sorry to bother") if they want directness.
4. strip jargon and corporate filler if they want human.
5. match their channel's norms, slack isn't email isn't a letter.
6. read it aloud once, if it sounds like a press release, loosen it.

name: Intro Request
description: asks someone to introduce you to a third party without being awkward
when_to_use: when the user wants to be connected to someone through a mutual contact
body:
1. make it easy by sending a forwardable blurb about yourself (3 sentences).
2. explain why this specific person is the right connection, not just 'i want to meet them'.
3. give the connector an out - 'only if it makes sense to you'.
4. keep the ask in one email, not a pre-ask then the real ask.
5. say what you'll do once connected so they're not left wondering.

name: Escalate Issue
description: writes an escalation message that moves things without burning bridges
when_to_use: when something is stuck and the user needs to involve a higher level without looking like they're complaining
body:
1. open with what you've already tried and when, no one should be surprised.
2. state the impact of continued inaction in concrete terms (cost, deadline, risk).
3. ask for a specific decision or action, not just attention.
4. keep it factual and calm, urgency is conveyed by facts not tone.
5. cc only the people who actually need to act or know.

name: Set Expectations
description: proactively defines scope, timeline, or availability to prevent misunderstandings
when_to_use: when the user is taking on work or starting something and wants to align upfront
body:
1. name what is and isn't included before anyone assumes.
2. give a realistic timeline with a single firm milestone.
3. say what you need from them to hit it.
4. flag the one most likely risk and how you'll handle it.
5. end by asking if this matches their expectations, not assuming it does.

name: Negotiate Timeline
description: pushes back on a deadline or scope without looking like you're dodging work
when_to_use: when the user has been given a timeline that doesn't work and needs to renegotiate
body:
1. acknowledge why the deadline matters to them first.
2. show what's already on your plate, data beats excuses.
3. offer a specific counter-date you can actually hit.
4. or offer a partial delivery by their date and the rest later.
5. frame it as 'here's how we get this done right' not 'i can't'.

name: Thank You Note
description: writes a specific and genuine thank-you that doesn't sound generic
when_to_use: when the user wants to thank someone for help, a gift, or a favor
body:
1. name the exact thing they did, not just 'thank you for everything'.
2. say what it meant or how it actually helped.
3. one detail that shows you noticed makes it real.
4. if there's a next step or you'd like to return the favor, say it.
5. keep it under 5 sentences, length doesn't equal sincerity.

name: Announce Change
description: communicates a process, team, or product change so people aren't blindsided
when_to_use: when the user needs to tell a group about something that's changing
body:
1. lead with what's changing and when, not the backstory.
2. say why in one line, people accept change better with a real reason.
3. tell them what stays the same so the change feels bounded.
4. list any action they need to take, if any.
5. give them a single contact or place to ask questions.
6. send before the change, not after.

name: Complaint Letter
description: writes a firm complaint that demands resolution without sounding unhinged
when_to_use: when the user wants to formally complain to a company or person and get results
body:
1. open with the facts: what happened, when, order/account numbers.
2. state the impact on you concisely.
3. say exactly what resolution you want (refund, replacement, action).
4. set a deadline for their response.
5. note any prior attempts to resolve it.
6. close professionally - you want results not revenge.

name: Reference Write
description: writes a strong recommendation or reference letter for someone
when_to_use: when the user is asked to write a reference or letter of recommendation
body:
1. open with how you know them and for how long.
2. pick two or three concrete strengths that fit the role they're going for.
3. back each one with a specific story or outcome, not adjectives.
4. address the biggest concern a reader might have preemptively if you can.
5. close with a clear endorsement and offer to be contacted.

name: Slack Message
description: writes a focused async message that gets a clear response
when_to_use: when the user needs to send a chat message that requires a decision or response
body:
1. front-load the ask or point, don't build up to it.
2. use one message not five, write it all then send.
3. bold the action item or question so it can't be skimmed past.
4. if it needs a decision, give 2-3 options to pick from.
5. say when you need it by, 'whenever you can' means never.

name: Pitch Idea
description: frames a new idea or proposal so it gets a fair hearing instead of an instant no
when_to_use: when the user wants to propose something to a manager, team, or client
body:
1. open with the problem you're solving, not the solution.
2. say why now, what makes this the right moment.
3. pitch the solution in 2-3 sentences, keep it crisp.
4. name the cost (time, money, effort) honestly, don't hide it.
5. say what success looks like and when you'd know if it's working.
6. end with the smallest possible first step to try it.
